focus: Research-backed patterns in how couples interact, fight, and connect. 50+ years of data on what predicts relationship success vs failure.
source_framework: Gottman (Four Horsemen, Sound Relationship House, Love Maps, bids for connection, masters vs disasters, 69% perpetual problems, 5:1 ratio, repair attempts)
watches_for: Four Horsemen: criticism (personality attack), contempt (superiority/sarcasm), defensiveness (counterattack), stonewalling (withdrawal)
Repair attempts -- who initiates, timing, whether it lands, pattern over sessions
Perpetual vs solvable -- is this the 69% being managed or the 31% being avoided?
Fighting to win vs fighting to understand -- masters understand, disasters win
Bids for connection -- turning towards, turning away, or turning against
Positive-to-negative ratio in interactions (stable = 5:1 or better)
Love Maps depth -- how well does each person know the other's inner world?
session_questions: Which Horsemen appeared? What antidotes were used or missed?
Were there repair attempts? Did they land? What's the trend across sessions?
Is this a perpetual problem being managed well, or a solvable problem being avoided?
What childhood/background history is driving this position? (90% rule)
Are the six gridlock questions being explored or is the couple stuck on surface content?
red_flags: Contempt present -- most corrosive predictor of divorce
Repair attempts consistently failing or not being made
Stonewalling pattern (one partner repeatedly withdraws)
Problems framed as personality defects ('you're thoughtless') vs situations
Couple claims they never argue -- what's being suppressed?
Fighting to win rather than understand

focus: The unconscious drivers beneath surface complaints. Childhood patterns repeating in adult relationships. Why you're really fighting about what you're fighting about.
source_framework: Guralnik (otherness as core challenge, conflicting loyalties, unconscious scripts, self-centeredness vs generosity, the atmosphere test, safety before solutions, stay in your own lane)
watches_for: Otherness reactions -- how does each person respond to their partner being different?
Unconscious scripts -- theories being assigned to partner to avoid discomfort ('she's shirking responsibility', 'he's lazy')
Conflicting loyalties -- family of origin vs partner, culture vs relationship, self vs couple
Projection -- putting own unresolved issues onto partner
Childhood pattern repetition -- whose parents are being replayed here?
Labels used as endpoints -- 'narcissist', 'gaslighting' used to stop thinking rather than start
Self-centeredness vs genuine curiosity about the other
session_questions: What is this REALLY about beneath the surface complaint?
Whose childhood is being replayed in this dynamic?
What conflicting loyalties are creating inner conflict for each person?
Where is each person being self-centered without realizing it?
Is each person curious about the other or building theories about why they're right?
What would happen if both people relaxed their convictions?
red_flags: One partner consistently externalizes all problems ('it's all your fault')
Labels used as investigation-stoppers ('he's a narcissist, case closed')
Sadistic/masochistic dynamic that neither can break
Partner treated as object to be changed rather than person to understand
Conditional love -- 'I'll love you when you change'
Zero curiosity about own role in the dynamic

focus: How early attachment patterns play out in adult relationships. The pursue-withdraw cycle. Creating secure base and safe haven.
source_framework: Bowlby (attachment theory), Johnson (EFT), Guralnik (safety before solutions, mine vs ours), Gottman (bids for connection as attachment behavior)
watches_for: Attachment styles activating -- anxious (pursue, seek reassurance), avoidant (withdraw, minimize), disorganized (oscillate)
Pursue-withdraw cycle -- one partner chases connection, the other retreats
Safety signals -- does each person feel safe enough to be vulnerable?
Mine vs ours tension -- where is the boundary between independence and interdependence?
Desire and being desired -- is each person feeling wanted?
Abandonment fears surfacing in conflict
The 'atmosphere' between the couple -- is it safe or threatening?
session_questions: Is each person feeling emotionally safe in this moment?
What attachment need is being expressed beneath the surface behavior?
Is there a pursue-withdraw cycle? Who is in which role? Does it ever flip?
What would each person need to feel secure enough to lower their defenses?
Is the couple creating conditions for each person's best self to emerge?
Where is fear driving behavior rather than love?
red_flags: One partner consistently pursuing while the other consistently withdraws -- stuck cycle
Vulnerability consistently punished or ignored
Overdependence on partner for all emotional needs (no other supports)
Complete emotional independence -- no vulnerability shared at all
Fear of abandonment driving controlling behavior
Desire/intimacy completely absent -- living in 'deadness'

focus: Relationships as vehicles for personal growth and mutual service. Tracking whether both individuals and the relationship are evolving.
source_framework: Shetty (growth and service vs pleasure and ease, home design metaphor, carrying each other in seasons, values honesty), Guralnik (capacity to love otherness as growth source, stinginess is destructive, compatibility overrated)
watches_for: Growth indicators -- is each person becoming a better human through this relationship?
Service without scorekeeping -- are they carrying each other or counting?
Values alignment vs values tolerance -- can they hold space for different values?
New iterations allowed -- does the relationship allow each person to change and evolve?
The home design test -- are they building something new together or replicating parents?
Generosity vs stinginess -- in attention, appreciation, effort, vulnerability
Therapist recommendations followed through or abandoned
session_questions: What has each person learned about themselves through this relationship recently?
Is either person waiting for the other to change before 'really' committing?
Are they building their own 'home' or replicating a parent's?
Is the relationship making both people more generous or more guarded?
What season is the relationship in? Who is carrying whom right now?
Are they growing together or growing apart? What's the evidence?
red_flags: Conditional love -- 'I'll be generous when you change'
Scorekeeping on who gave more, did more, sacrificed more
One person expected to abandon their values/identity for the relationship
No growth visible across multiple sessions -- stuck
Relationship framed as pleasure/comfort delivery system rather than growth partnership
Stinginess with appreciation, attention, or vulnerability

focus: Identity within relationship. The tension between closeness and selfhood. Aliveness vs deadness. Love vs desire. How emotional history shapes relational and sexual patterns.
source_framework: Perel (mating in captivity, identity and change, curiosity vs reactivity, love and desire as separate systems, erotic blueprints, repair vs revival, cornerstone vs capstone, fundamental attribution error, confirmation bias in couples)
watches_for: Identity negotiation -- is each person maintaining selfhood while allowing closeness?
Abandonment vs suffocation dynamic -- who fears losing the other, who fears losing themselves?
Curiosity vs reactivity -- engaging with the unknown or escalating in defense?
Stories mistaken for facts -- subjective narratives treated as objective reality
Aliveness vs deadness -- is the relationship vibrant or just surviving?
Love-desire split -- can they integrate intimacy and desire or are these compartmentalized?
Confirmation bias and fundamental attribution error in conflict
session_questions: Is this couple alive or just not dead?
Who fears losing the other vs losing themselves? How does this play out?
Where is reactivity replacing curiosity?
What story has each person confused with fact?
What parts of themselves has each person lost or gained through this relationship?
If this couple were to have a second marriage with each other, what would it look like?
red_flags: One person has completely subsumed their identity into the relationship
Total absence of playfulness, curiosity, or novelty -- living in deadness
Partner treated as someone to be changed rather than discovered
Self-flagellation replacing genuine empathy (narcissistic shame spiral)
Repair without revival -- couple survives but never regenerates
Complete separation of love and desire

focus: The operational mechanics of relationship skills. Which part of you is showing up? Whats the adaptive child doing? How do you get into wise adult and stay there?
source_framework: Real (wise adult vs adaptive child, one-up/one-down, harmony-disharmony-repair, relational mindfulness, three steps, no harshness, relational vs individual empowerment, marry your unfinished business)
watches_for: Which part is showing up -- wise adult or adaptive child?
One-up behavior: grandiosity, righteousness, attacking, contempt
One-down behavior: fixing, pleasing, resentful accommodation, shutting down
The loop: more A does X, more B does Y, repeat
Harshness -- in any direction (to partner, from partner, to self)
Individual empowerment disguised as growth
Repair actually happening or just cycling harmony-disharmony
session_questions: Who showed up -- wise adults or adaptive children?
Whats the loop? The more [A does X], the more [B does Y]?
Where is the adaptive child running the show? What childhood pattern repeats?
Is there harshness? What would loving firmness look like instead?
Is repair actually happening or just cycling?
What would it look like for the one-up to get small and the one-down to get big?
red_flags: Adaptive child running the relationship with no wise adult check-in
The loop is accelerating -- more fighting/shutting down each session
Harshness normalized (thats just how I am)
Individual empowerment without relational empowerment
No repair -- just cycling harmony and disharmony endlessly
One person consistently one-up, other consistently one-down -- frozen roles
